dossier.overview:
**Overworld NPC AI** is the architecture for **free-roaming actors** on Resort maps: guest Pokémon, staff Pokémon, and trainer NPCs that are **not** attached to the player. Maps and smart objects publish **activity offers**; each actor runs hard filters, soft scoring, and weighted random selection. No monolithic brain, no per-species decision trees, no full life sim.

**Out of scope for this idea:** partner Pokémon that follow the player, temporary follow bubbles, and Nature idle when the player stands still. Those live in the separate **Partner idle behaviour** idea and the shipped follower prototype.

Core pattern: **the world offers activities; free NPCs choose from those offers** using Nature, traits, relationships, map context, and randomness. Smart objects, zones, nearby actors, staff roles, and the player publish possible activities.

Runtime goal: believable personalities with **predictable, efficient** per-map simulation (hot, warm, cold tiers). Canonical dev handoff: `pokemon-resort/docs/gameplay/overworld_pokemon_architecture_handoff.md`.

sections.title: System overview
sections.summary: Affordance-based NPC AI: providers, offers, filter, score, execute.

blocks.body:
Design goals:

• **Feel alive, stay cheap**: only the current map is fully simulated; adjacent maps activate near connectors; unloaded maps keep snapshots.
• **Data-driven content**: smart objects and zones are the main authoring unit; designers define offers and sequences, not C++ per object.
• **Global animation primitives**: sprite-based Pokémon reuse walk, face, wait, jump, emote, sleep; balls and props animate as overlays.
• **Durable identity**: `pkrid` everywhere; never box index; one Pokémon in one place at a time.
• **Ambient offers**: not every actor should be dramatic; default stand/wander activities compete in the same selector.

blocks.title: End-to-end mental model
blocks.caption: Resort data defines who; the simulation decides what they do on a map
blocks.source:
flowchart TB
    RD["Resort data pkrid species nature"] --> PR["PokemonPresenceRegistry"]
    PR --> MAP["MapPokemonSimulation"]
    ZONES["Spawn zones"] --> MAP
    SO["Smart objects"] --> MAP
    MAP --> ACT["PokemonActor"]
    ACT --> SEL["Filter score pick"]
    SEL --> EX["ActivityExecutor"]
    MAP --> SNAP["MapSnapshotStore"]
    SNAP --> REL["ReloadPredictionResolver"]
    MEM["Memories and friendship"] -.-> SEL

blocks.title: Affordance selection pipeline
blocks.caption: Collect offers, filter, score, reserve, execute
blocks.source:
flowchart LR
    P1["ActivityProviders"] --> OFF["ActivityOffers"]
    OFF --> FIL["Hard filters"]
    FIL --> SCR["Soft scoring"]
    SCR --> WRS["Weighted random pick"]
    WRS --> RES["ReservationManager"]
    RES --> HS["HandshakeCoordinator"]
    HS --> EXEC["ActivityExecutor"]

sections.title: Actor scope
sections.summary: Free-roaming map actors only. Same decision pipeline for all three.

blocks.html:
ActorBindingDecision loop
Guest PokémonOne map at a time via pkrid; migration is an explicit activityActivity offers from zones, smart objects, nearby actors
Staff PokémonJob anchor zone (pool, café, garden)Same selector; staff role provider adds work offers with higher weight
Trainer NPCMap-authored human overworld characterSame selector; can use simple smart objects (bench sit, inspect)

Not covered here: partner follow, temporary follow, player-attached idle. See Partner idle behaviour.

blocks.body: All free actors share `MapPokemonSimulation`, `ActivitySelector`, and `ActivityExecutor`. **Do not** route guest or staff logic through `FollowerController`. Partner systems are player-attached; this idea is map-bound autonomy.

sections.title: Map simulation tiers
sections.summary: Hot, warm, and cold maps; presence states; reload prediction.

blocks.title: Simulation tiers
blocks.caption: Only hot maps update every frame; warm retains detail; cold is compact presence
blocks.source:
flowchart TD
    HOT["Hot map: full sim every frame"] -->|player leaves| WARM["Warm map: snapshot retained 300s"]
    WARM -->|player returns soon| HOT
    WARM -->|retention expired| COLD["Cold map: compact presence only"]
    COLD -->|reload| HOT

blocks.body: Adjacent maps become hot only when the player is near a connector (entrance, edge, warp).

blocks.caption: Default map simulation tier config (target shape)

blocks.html:
Every free-roam Resort Pokémon has one presenceState. Live actors are projections; destroy on unload, rebuild from snapshot or spawn decision on load.

StateMeaning
available_offstageIn Resort, not on a public map (room, rest, private wander)
guest_on_map / staff_on_mapActive on a specific map, running this AI loop
migrating_between_mapsExplicit migration activity in progress
warm_snapshot / cold_snapshotUnloaded map memory
away_in_game / pending_returnTransfer / OpenHome presence (existing Resort model)

blocks.caption: Identity rule from current handoff
blocks.body:
Critical identity rule: pkrid is the durable Resort identity.
Overworld runtime state must treat live actors as projections of current Resort records, not as permanent owners of Pokemon data.
When a map becomes hot again, `elapsedSeconds = now - snapshot.lastUpdatedAt` drives believable continuity.

blocks.html:
ElapsedBehavior
0 to 20 sResume activity exactly (same ball pass, same step)
20 to 90 sPredict position within activity timeline
90 to 180 sComplete activity offscreen; relationship and memory hooks
180 to 300 sFast-forward to ambient placement in zone
300 to 900 sReroll activities; same Pokémon may still be on map
900 s or morePopulation refresh allowed (swap guests, offstage moves)

blocks.caption: Reload prediction thresholds (seconds)

blocks.title: Unload and reload
blocks.caption: Snapshot is compact, not per-frame animation state
blocks.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Hot as HotMap
    participant Snap as SnapshotStore
    participant Reg as PresenceRegistry
    Hot->>Snap: On unload save per pkrid
    Hot->>Reg: Update presence state
    Note over Hot: Destroy PokemonActor
    Snap->>Hot: On load pass elapsedSeconds
    Hot->>Hot: ReloadPredictionResolver
    Hot->>Hot: Resume predict complete or reroll

sections.title: Activity system
sections.summary: Offers, filters, scoring, reservations, and global primitives.

blocks.body:
**ActivityProvider** sources: map zone, smart object, nearby Pokémon, nearby NPC, player, staff role, default self behavior, memory/favorite-place provider, migration provider.

Each returns **ActivityOffer** records with `activityId`, `baseWeight`, `tags`, `requiredRoles`, `durationSeconds`, `minimumDurationSeconds`, and cooldowns.

blocks.html:
Main runtime components (target modules, not one file):

  • PokemonPresenceRegistry: one pkrid, one place
  • MapPokemonSimulation: per hot map
  • PokemonActor: runtime-only; no permanent memories on actor
  • ActivityFilter / ActivityScorer / ActivitySelector
  • ReservationManager / HandshakeCoordinator
  • ActivityExecutor + SmartObjectRuntime
  • MapSnapshotStore / ReloadPredictionResolver / MigrationPlanner

blocks.title: Multi-actor handshake
blocks.caption: Two Pokémon passing a ball must reserve roles before animating
blocks.source:
flowchart TD
    A["Initiator picks ball_two_pokemon_pass"] --> B["ReservationManager checks tiles roles object"]
    B --> C["Ask candidate participant"]
    C --> D{"All accept"}
    D -->|yes| E["Commit reservation and run ActivityExecutor"]
    D -->|no| F["Release all reservations retry cooldown"]

blocks.body:
**Hard filters** remove impossible offers: object occupied, no path, wrong size, type forbidden in zone, cooldown, map at capacity.

**Soft score** is clamped 0 to 100, then weighted random selection (not always the highest score). Nature and characteristic tables bias which offers win (assertive, steady, curious, composed, energetic, neutral). Characteristics like "Takes plenty of naps" or "Highly curious" are **data tables**, not hardcoded.

blocks.caption: Soft scoring inputs (target shape)
blocks.body:
{
  "activityScoring": {
    "terms": [
      "baseWeight",
      "natureScore",
      "characteristicScore",
      "speciesScore",
      "friendshipScore",
      "memoryScore",
      "staffRoleScore",
      "mapZoneScore",
      "repetitionPenalty",
      "randomnessJitter"
    ],
    "scoreClampMin": 0,
    "scoreClampMax": 100,
    "randomJitterMin": -5,
    "randomJitterMax": 5,
    "selection": "weighted_random"
  }
}

blocks.html:
Default ambient offers (self-provider; prevent overactivity). These are normal activities in the selector, not a separate idle system:

ActivityBase weight
idle_stand25
idle_look_around30
wander_small_radius35
idle_watch_others20
idle_nap15

StateNotes
candidate → reserved → starting → activeMinimum duration before natural end
minimum_time_reached → ending → completeOr cancelled / failed

Recommended minimums: idle 2s, wander 4s, inspect 4s, nap 10s, play 8s, ball game 10s, staff work 8s.

blocks.body: Activities compose **global animation primitives** only. No per-species ball sprites. The **ball object** runs arc and bounce; Pokémon only face, emote, and nudge.

blocks.caption: Global animation primitives (shared across all smart objects)
blocks.body:
walk_to
face_direction
face_target
wait
jump
hop_to_tile
sprite_nudge_forward
sprite_nudge_back
emote
sleep
wake
circle_target
return_to_anchor
soft_snap_if_needed

sections.title: Smart objects and zones
sections.summary: Map objects that publish activities. The Sims is the reference model; Resort maps use the same pattern for Pokémon and trainer NPCs.

blocks.body:
A **smart object** is a map-placed thing that **publishes activity offers** to nearby NPCs. It owns:

• which activities are available (`sit`, `cook`, `play`, `inspect`, …)
• role points (where the actor stands and faces)
• requirements (size, type, occupancy)
• animation sequences built from **global primitives**
• cooldowns and completion effects

The NPC does **not** embed object logic. It queries providers, scores offers, and runs the sequence the object defines. Scenery with no offers is not a smart object.

blocks.caption: The Sims 1: canonical smart-object model
blocks.body:
In *The Sims*, the **pool** publishes *Dive* and *Swim*. The **stove** publishes *Cook*. The **bookcase** publishes *Read*. The **fish tank** publishes *Watch*. Sims (NPCs) score those offers and walk to the object; the object drives the interaction animation.

Resort **Overworld NPC AI** uses the same split: smart objects publish, Pokémon and trainer NPCs pick. We add map simulation tiers (hot/warm/cold) so we do not simulate every room every frame.

blocks.title: Object publishes, actor executes
blocks.caption: Logic lives on the object, not inside PokemonActor
blocks.source:
flowchart LR
    OBJ["Smart object data"] -->|offers| PROV["ActivityProvider"]
    ACT["Pokemon or trainer NPC"] -->|query| PROV
    ACT -->|score and pick| SEL["ActivitySelector"]
    SEL -->|reserve| RES["ReservationManager"]
    RES -->|run| EXEC["ActivityExecutor"]
    OBJ -->|overlay motion| EXEC

blocks.body:
Not every smart object needs a long sequence. **Simple objects** publish one or two offers with a single role point. A **bench** tells any valid actor: you can **sit here**.

Both **trainer NPCs** (human overworld characters) and **Pokémon** can accept the same `bench_sit` offer if hard filters pass (path clear, seat free, size allowed). The bench does not care which species sits; it only exposes the affordance.

blocks.caption: Benches as simple smart objects (Pokémon Black reference)
blocks.body:
Three benches in a park zone. In Resort authoring, each bench would be a smart object with offers such as `bench_sit` (rest, 20 to 60s) and maybe `bench_social` when two actors are nearby. Trainer NPCs standing with their Pokémon are separate actors; either could use an empty bench independently.

The fenced zone around them is a **spawn zone** (capacity, roam radius), not the bench itself.

blocks.caption: Simple bench smart object (single role, one sit offer)
Interior props as smart objects (Platinum villa reference)
blocks.body:
Player villa interior from *Platinum*: kitchen counter, table, chairs, TV, piano, bookshelves, bed. In the original game most of these are scenery. In Resort they would be authored as smart objects where gameplay needs them:

• **chair / sofa** → `sit`, `nap_if_allowed`
• **bed** → `rest` (longer duration)
• **piano / TV** → `inspect`, `idle_watch` (optional emote)
• **kitchen counter** → staff-only `prepare_food` during work shifts

Same room, same tiles; data defines which props publish offers.

**Interactive smart objects** run multi-step sequences and may involve overlays (balls, food) or multiple actors (two Pokémon passing a ball). The Pokémon sprite stays generic; the object handles prop motion.

blocks.caption: Ball smart object: solo play sequence
blocks.body:
Gen 4-style reference: Chimchar balances a ball on its head. In Resort the **ball** is the smart object. It publishes `ball_solo_bounce` with approach points and a sequence (`face_object`, `jump`, `sprite_nudge_forward`). The ball overlay handles bounce timing; Chimchar only runs global primitives.

Designer workflow: object id, tags, approach points, role points, offers, requirements, cooldowns, completion effects. No custom C++ per object.

blocks.caption: Two-Pokémon beach ball activity offer (data shape only)

blocks.body: Smart objects need a **declarative lifecycle** for authoring and a **timeline projection** API for map reload. Goal: when the player enters a map, some Pokémon are already mid-activity (sitting, mid ball pass, about to stand up), not walking in from a cold spawn every time.

blocks.html:
HookWhen it runsTypical use
onEnterActivity starts; actor reached role pointOccupy slot, play latch anim, enable object overlay
onStepEach sequence step beginsBall toss, emote, sound cue
onExitActivity ending (natural or forced)Release reservations, stand up, clear overlay
onCompleteAfter onExit when finished normallyFriendship, memory chance, object cooldown
onCancelInterrupted (player warp, handshake fail)Safe cleanup, short retry cooldown

Hooks are data on the activity offer, not C++ per object. ActivityExecutor dispatches them; the smart object definition lists which effects fire.

blocks.title: Map reload: cold spawn vs mid-activity
blocks.caption: ReloadPredictionResolver picks per actor from snapshot + elapsedSeconds
blocks.source:
flowchart TD
    LOAD["Map becomes hot"] --> ELAPSED["elapsedSeconds = now - snapshot.lastUpdatedAt"]
    ELAPSED --> HAS{"Snapshot has active activity?"}
    HAS -->|no| COLD["Ambient spawn wander or idle offer"]
    HAS -->|yes| PROJ["SmartObjectRuntime.projectActivityElapsed"]
    PROJ --> PHASE{"Projected phase"}
    PHASE -->|entering| ENTER["Skip walk-in place at enter pose"]
    PHASE -->|in_progress| MID["Join mid-sequence at step + overlay state"]
    PHASE -->|exiting| EXIT["About to leave object post-activity"]
    PHASE -->|complete| DONE["onComplete already applied offscreen"]

blocks.body: **`projectActivityElapsed`** is the primary method for joining an activity already in progress after simulated time away. Called on `SmartObjectRuntime`; orchestrated by `ReloadPredictionResolver` on map load.

blocks.caption: projectActivityElapsed (API sketch)
Activity offer with lifecycle hooks and elapsed projection flags
Unload snapshot: enough to project mid-activity on return
blocks.body:
{
  "pkrid": "pkr_000123",
  "mapId": "beach_01",
  "activity": {
    "activityId": "ball_two_pokemon_pass",
    "state": "in_progress",
    "smartObjectId": "beach_ball_01",
    "sequenceStep": 4,
    "startedAt": 123430.0,
    "plannedDurationSeconds": 28.0,
    "activitySeed": 882144
  },
  "lastUpdatedAt": 123456.0
}

blocks.caption: Result of projectActivityElapsed (player away 35s, activity still running)

blocks.html:
Authoring rules for elapsed projection

  • Every projectable step needs a durationSeconds (or derivable from repeat count) so the timeline can be walked without replaying frames.
  • Mark looping steps (object_arc_between_roles) with how many repeats fit in plannedDurationSeconds; activitySeed picks the repeat count at start so offscreen projection stays deterministic.
  • entering steps are skipped on reload when skipWalkIn: true; actors snap to role points in pose.
  • exiting phase: player may see Pokémon standing up or walking away from the bench as if activity just ended.
  • Activities with elapsedProjectable: false fall back to complete-offscreen or ambient placement after threshold.

blocks.body:
Player experience targets:

• **Brief absence (under 20s):** resume exact step.
• **Medium absence:** `projectActivityElapsed` lands in `in_progress` (mid sit, mid pass, mid inspect).
• **Longer absence:** phase may be `exiting` or `complete`; some actors already left the object, others still winding down.
• **Mix on one map:** not every actor projects; some cold-start ambient offers so the scene is not synchronized.

blocks.html:
Authoring unitPurposeExample
Smart objectPublishes activities, role points, sequencesBench, ball, pool edge, piano
Spawn zoneWho can appear, capacity, roam radius, zone tagsPool guest zone, villa interior
Exclusion zoneBlock placement and pathingDoorways, warps, player spawn
Smart nodeLightweight POI without a full object meshlook_at_ocean, sit_at_pool_edge

Future owmap or sidecar metadata extension; not in owmap v1 today. See owmap-format docs and handoff map spawn contract gap.

blocks.body: Common Resort smart object types to author: `ball`, `pool`, `chair`, `bench`, `toy`, `food_bowl`, `garden_patch`, `stage`, `fountain`, `photo_spot`, `training_dummy`, `beach_umbrella`, `staff_counter`.

sections.title: Memories, friendship, migration
sections.summary: Curated tokens, not an event log; purposeful map moves.

blocks.body:
**Memories** are curated tokens (`played_ball_together`, `napped_near_friend`, `received_photo`, …) with participants, mapId, smartObjectId, importance, tone. Photo gifts can link to a treasured memory for interaction flavor without speech.

**Friendship graph** keyed by `pkrid` biases spawn-together and social scoring; it does not force behavior.

**Migration** is an explicit activity (go_to_friend, go_to_favorite_place, wander_to_nearby_map), capacity-aware, max hops configurable. If the player does not follow and the Pokémon is 2+ maps away, offscreen complete or offstage chance applies.

blocks.title: Player approach flow
blocks.caption: At most one Pokémon approaching the player at once; opt-out required
blocks.source:
flowchart LR
    SEE["Player nearby"] --> SCORE["Score approach offer"]
    SCORE --> SLOT["Reserve interaction slot"]
    SLOT --> APP["Walk and emote"]
    APP --> WAIT{"Player responds"}
    WAIT -->|yes| INT["Dialogue or minigame"]
    WAIT -->|timeout| LEAVE["Return to wander"]

sections.title: Development phases
sections.summary: Six phases for free-roam NPC decision architecture. Reservations before multi-actor play.

blocks.html:
PhaseBuildSuccess criterion
1Presence registry, spawn zones, guest actors, unload/reload snapshots, ambient wander offersFree Pokémon appear from Resort data and survive map transitions
2Activity offers, filters, scoring, Nature/characteristic tablesDifferent actors pick different activities on the same map
3Smart objects, solo sequences, global primitivesChairs, balls, pool edges, toys work
4Reservations, handshakes, multi-actor activitiesBall pass without desync
5Map graph, migration, offstagePurposeful leave and later believable arrival
6Friendship, memory tokens, photo giftsMeaningful events bias future offers

blocks.title: Phase dependency
blocks.caption: Linear build order for free-roam NPC AI
blocks.source:
flowchart LR
    P1["Phase 1 presence"] --> P2["Phase 2 selector"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase 3 smart objects"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase 4 handshakes"]
    P4 --> P5["Phase 5 migration"]
    P5 --> P6["Phase 6 memories"]

blocks.html:
• Do not build one giant Pokémon controller.
  • Do not put smart object logic inside Pokémon actors.
  • Do not simulate unloaded maps every frame.
  • Do not let live actors own permanent Resort data.
  • Do not require species-specific animations for shared activities.
  • Do not use box slots as identity.
  • Do not allow one pkrid in two maps at once.
  • Do not let every Pokémon approach the player at once.
  • Do not make migration random noise; it must feel purposeful.
  • Do not store memories as a raw event log.
  • Do not end activities instantly unless cancelled.

items.label: Pokémon memory system (durable history per pkrid)
owmap format (terrain and map metadata today)

blocks.body:
In-repo targets for implementation docs:

• `pokemon-resort/docs/gameplay/overworld_pokemon_life_system.md` (full architecture draft)
• `pokemon-resort/docs/gameplay/overworld_pokemon_architecture_handoff.md` (current code boundaries)

Staging folder for behaviour captures: `public/media/npc-behaviour/` (gifs, sprites, screenshots).
